learn.title: Green Steel Production with Hydrogen
learn.intro: Traditional steelmaking relies heavily on coal, contributing nearly 8% of global CO2 emissions. Replacing coal with hydrogen in blast furnaces offers a pathway to decarbonize one of the world's most emission-intensive industries, making green steel essential for clean energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Direct Reduction Reduction (DR) processes use hydrogen instead of coal to produce sponge iron. This technology can reduce CO2 emissions by up to 95% compared to traditional blast furnaces. The process involves heating iron ore with hydrogen-rich gases at temperatures around 1,000°C.
learn.c2_text: Integrating hydrogen into existing blast furnaces requires careful blending strategies. Currently, up to 20% hydrogen can be safely injected without major modifications. The reaction produces water vapor instead of CO2, fundamentally changing the emission profile of steel production.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MMT of green hydrogen-based steel annually by 2030. Steel Authority of India (SAIL) plans to transition to hydrogen-based DRI by 2027. The country aims to become a global hub for green steel exports with domestic production costs competitive by 2030.
learn.stat1: Global steel production: 1.9 billion tonnes annually (2024)
learn.stat2: Hydrogen-based steel reduces CO2 emissions by 95% vs coal-based methods
learn.stat3: India targets 5 million metric tons of green steel by 2030
learn.stat4: Traditional steelmaking accounts for 8% of global CO2 emissions

learn.title: Smart Grids and Advanced Metering
learn.intro: As renewable energy sources become more variable, power systems need intelligent digital infrastructure to balance supply and demand. Smart grids with advanced metering enable two-way communication, real-time monitoring, and automated control, making the energy transition more reliable and efficient.
learn.c1_text: Smart meters transmit energy usage data from homes and businesses to utilities in real-time, while also receiving signals for demand response programs. This bidirectional flow enables dynamic pricing, outage detection within minutes, and integration of distributed solar and storage systems across the grid.
learn.c2_text: Advanced metering infrastructure uses IoT sensors and automated switches to balance supply from renewables with fluctuating demand. Machine learning algorithms predict consumption patterns and optimize grid operations, reducing outages by up to 30% and enabling renewable energy penetration above 50% in modern grids.
learn.c3_text: India's Smart Grid Mission aims to deploy 250 million smart meters by 2025. The Delhi Metro Rail Corporation uses smart grid technology to integrate 350 MW of rooftop solar. The government plans to establish 25 Smart Grid Demonstration Projects across states to showcase scalable solutions for urban and rural electrification.
learn.stat1: Smart meters deployed globally: over 800 million units (2024)
learn.stat2: Advanced metering reduces electricity theft by up to 15%
learn.stat3: India targets 250 million smart meters by 2025
learn.stat4: Smart grids can reduce outage duration by 30%

learn.title: Wind Turbine Blade Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines typically last 20-25 years, after which thousands of blades made from composite materials face disposal challenges. Developing recycling technologies for these large composite structures is crucial for sustainable wind energy growth and circular economy principles in the renewable sector.
learn.c1_text: Wind turbine blades are made from glass or carbon fiber embedded in epoxy resin composites. These materials are strong yet lightweight, but difficult to recycle using conventional methods. A typical blade weighs 1-3 tonnes and contains 30-50% fiber by weight, requiring specialized thermal or chemical decomposition processes.
learn.c2_text: Mechanical recycling grinds blades into fiberglass powder for use in construction materials. Pyrolysis involves heating blades to 500°C without oxygen, breaking down epoxy into oil or gas. These processes can recover 70-90% of blade materials, though challenges remain in handling carbon fiber blades which require more intensive processing.
learn.c3_text: India has over 40 GW of wind capacity with first major blade retirements expected by 2025. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy is developing recycling guidelines and partnering with manufacturers like Vestas and Siemens Gamesa. Tamil Nadu's policy mandates blade recycling for projects seeking renewable purchase obligations, setting a precedent for national implementation.
learn.stat1: Global wind blade waste projected: 2.8 million tonnes by 2030
learn.stat2: Wind turbines have 20-25 year operational lifespan

learn.title: EV Charging Infrastructure Types
learn.intro: Electric vehicles require different charging speeds depending on usage patterns - from overnight home charging to rapid highway charging. Understanding Level 1, Level 2, and DC fast charging helps consumers choose appropriate infrastructure and supports widespread EV adoption through optimized charging networks.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses standard 120V household outlets, adding 2-5 miles of range per hour of charging. Most common for overnight charging at homes, it's sufficient for daily driving under 40 miles. While universal and affordable, it's too slow for long-distance travel or fleet operations requiring quick turnaround.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 operates at 240V, delivering 10-60 miles of range per hour - completing a full charge overnight. DC fast charging uses 400-800V systems, adding 100-200 miles in 30 minutes. Tesla Superchargers and Electrify America stations use this technology, enabling cross-country travel with minimal stops.
learn.c3_text: India's Atal Jyoti Yojana plans 25,000 charging stations by 2025, focusing on highways and urban areas. The government mandates DC fast chargers every 25 km on national highways. Tamil Nadu leads with 1,200 public chargers, while Ola and Ather Energy expand Level 2 infrastructure in cities like Bangalore and Chennai for last-mile mobility.
learn.stat2: Level 1 adds 2-5 miles range per hour
learn.stat3: DC fast charging adds 100-200 miles in 30 minutes

learn.title: Green Bonds and Climate Finance
learn.intro: Green bonds mobilize capital specifically for environmentally beneficial projects, including renewable energy, energy efficiency, and clean transportation. These financial instruments have grown exponentially, providing crucial funding for the global energy transition while offering investors transparent, measurable environmental impact.
learn.c1_title: Bond Structure and Use of Proceeds 📊
learn.c1_text: Green bonds are fixed-income securities where proceeds fund eligible climate and environmental projects. Unlike conventional bonds, they require external verification and annual reporting on project allocation and impact metrics. The global market reached $525 billion in 2023, with renewable energy projects receiving approximately 40% of total proceeds.
learn.c2_text: Green bonds operate within broader climate finance frameworks that include sustainability-linked loans and ESG investing. Project evaluation uses standardized criteria like the Green Bond Principles, ensuring additionality and measurable outcomes. Greenhouse gas reductions, certified by third parties, determine ongoing compliance and investor transparency requirements.
learn.c3_text: India issued $13 billion in green bonds since 2020, including $2.5 billion sovereign green bonds in 2023. The Reserve Bank of India has registered 45 green bond issuances worth ₹1.2 trillion. Indian renewable companies like Adani Green and Tata Solar have tapped international markets for $1.8 billion combined, demonstrating strong investor confidence in the country's clean energy transition.
learn.stat2: Renewable energy receives 40% of green bond proceeds
learn.stat3: India's green bonds issuance: $13 billion since 2020
learn.stat4: RBI registered ₹1.2 trillion in green bonds

learn.title: Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE)
learn.intro: LCOE provides a standardized method to compare the total cost of electricity generation across different technologies, accounting for capital expenses, operations, fuel, and financing. This crucial metric has shown solar and wind becoming cheaper than fossil fuels in most markets, driving the global energy transition.
learn.c1_text: LCOE equals total lifetime costs divided by total energy produced: (Capital Costs + Fuel + O&M) / Lifetime Energy Output. For renewables, fuel costs are zero, while fossil plants include coal, gas, and carbon pricing. This metric enables apples-to-apples comparisons between solar, wind, nuclear, and gas plants regardless of technology differences.
learn.c2_text: Solar PV costs fell 85% between 2010-2023, while onshore wind dropped 60%. Learning rates show costs decline 20-25% for every doubling of installed capacity. As of 2024, global weighted-average LCOE is $26/MWh for solar, $44/MWh for wind, compared to $120/MWh for new coal and $60/MWh for new gas in emerging markets.
learn.c3_text: India set global records with solar auctions at ₹2.00/kWh ($0.024/kWh) in 2023, among world's lowest prices. Wind tariffs reached ₹3.00/kWh in Tamil Nadu auctions. The country's solar capacity grew to 70 GW (2024), while wind reached 42.5 GW. These low costs enabled India to achieve grid parity 5 years ahead of schedule, making renewables the cheapest new capacity source.

learn.title: Ocean Energy Technologies
learn.intro: Ocean energy encompasses multiple technologies harnessing marine environments for clean power generation. From established tidal barrage systems to emerging floating offshore wind and wave energy converters, these technologies offer predictable renewable energy sources with high potential for coastal and island nations seeking energy independence.
learn.c1_text: Tidal barrages use barriers across estuaries to generate power from predictable tidal flows, similar to hydroelectric dams. Wave energy converters capture kinetic energy from ocean surface waves using floating devices or submerged buoys. Current technology readiness ranges from demonstration scale to commercial deployment, with Europe leading in wave energy innovation.
learn.c2_text: Floating offshore wind turbines access deeper waters beyond fixed-bottom limitations, opening 60% of global offshore resources. These platforms use mooring systems and spars to keep turbines stable in waters 200-1,000 meters deep. The technology achieved cost parity with fixed-bottom wind in 2023, with commercial projects now operating in Scotland, Norway, and Japan.
learn.c3_text: India's 7,500 km coastline has 30,000 MW tidal potential and 100,000 MW wave energy potential. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy identified 17 sites for ocean energy development. Gujarat's Muppandal region hosts India's first floating solar-wind hybrid project, while the National Institute of Ocean Technology tests tidal turbines in the Gulf of Khambhat. India aims for 500 MW ocean energy capacity by 2030.
learn.stat2: Floating offshore wind opens 60% of global offshore resources
learn.stat3: Gujarat's Muppandal: India's first hybrid project
learn.stat4: India targets 500 MW ocean energy by 2030
